epigraph.text: Test everything; hold fast what is good.

content.text:
Given a series $\sum a_{n}$, how do we determine whether it converges or
diverges? For geometric and telescoping series, we can compute partial sums
explicitly. But most series resist such direct attacks. We need indirect
methods—**convergence tests**—that determine convergence from the
form of the terms $a_{n}$.
Our first test is simple but important: it catches "obvious" divergence.

content.title: The Divergence Test (nth Term Test)
content.content:
If $\displaystyle\limto{n\to\infty}a_{n} \neq 0$ or does not exist, then
$\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_{n}$ diverges.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We prove the contrapositive: if $\sum a_{n}$ converges, then
$\limto{n\to\infty}a_{n} = 0$.

Suppose $\sum a_{n} = S$. Then the partial sums $s_{n} \to S$. Since
$a_{n} = s_{n} - s_{n-1}$, we have
\[
 \limto{n\to\infty}a_{n}
 = \limto{n\to\infty}(s_{n} - s_{n-1})
 = S - S = 0.
\]

content.title: The Divergence Test cannot prove convergence
content.content:
The Divergence Test is a *one-way test*: it can prove divergence but
*never* convergence. If $\limto{n\to\infty}a_{n} = 0$, the series
*might* converge or *might* diverge. The harmonic series
$\sum 1/n$ is the classic example: the terms go to zero, but the series
diverges.

content.title: Applying the Divergence Test
content.solution:
\[
 \limto{n\to\infty}\frac{n}{n+1}
 = \limto{n\to\infty}\frac{1}{1+1/n} = 1 \neq 0.
\]
By the Divergence Test, the series **diverges**.

content.title: Divergence Test with oscillating terms
content.solution:
The terms are $-1, 1, -1, 1, \ldots$. The limit $\limto{n\to\infty}(-1)^{n}$
does not exist. By the Divergence Test, the series **diverges**.

content.title: More Divergence Test applications
content.problem:
Use the Divergence Test to determine whether each series diverges.

**(a)** $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{n^{2}}{n^{2}+1}$

**(b)** $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{n!}{2^{n}}$

**(c)** $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}e^{-n}$
content.solution:
**(a)** $\limto{n\to\infty}\frac{n^{2}}{n^{2}+1} = 1 \neq 0$. 
**Diverges** by the Divergence Test.

**(b)** For large $n$, $n! \gg 2^{n}$ (factorials grow faster than 
exponentials), so $\frac{n!}{2^{n}} \to \infty \neq 0$. 
**Diverges** by the Divergence Test.

**(c)** $\limto{n\to\infty}e^{-n} = 0$. The Divergence Test is 
**inconclusive**. (This series actually converges—it's geometric 
with $r = 1/e < 1$.)

content.title: When the Divergence Test is inconclusive
content.solution:
\[
 \limto{n\to\infty}\frac{1}{n^{2}} = 0.
\]
The Divergence Test tells us nothing. We need another test to determine
convergence. (We will soon show this series converges.)

content.title: Subtle application of the Divergence Test
content.solution:
The terms $\sin 1, \sin 2, \sin 3, \ldots$ do not approach any limit—they 
oscillate densely through $[-1, 1]$ without settling down. (This is 
because $\pi$ is irrational, so the sequence $\{n \mod 2\pi\}$ is 
equidistributed in $[0, 2\pi)$.)

Since $\limto{n\to\infty}\sin n$ does not exist, the Divergence Test tells 
us the series **diverges**.

content.text:
The Integral Test connects series to the improper integrals we studied in
Chapter 8. It is one of our most powerful tools.

content.title: The Integral Test
content.content:
Let $f$ be a continuous, positive, decreasing function on $[1,\infty)$ with
$f(n) = a_{n}$ for all positive integers $n$. Then:
\[
 \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}a_{n}\text{ converges}
 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
 \defint{1}{\infty}f(x)\dx\text{ converges}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Consider the rectangles with height $a_{n}$ and width $1$ for
$n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$. Since $f$ is decreasing:

- Right-endpoint rectangles: The sum $\sum_{n=2}^{N}a_{n}$ is a lower
 bound for $\defint{1}{N}f(x)\dx$.

- Left-endpoint rectangles: The sum $\sum_{n=1}^{N-1}a_{n}$ is an upper
 bound for $\defint{1}{N}f(x)\dx$.
Thus:
\[
 \sum_{n=2}^{N}a_{n} \leq \defint{1}{N}f(x)\dx \leq \sum_{n=1}^{N-1}a_{n}.
\]
Taking $N \to \infty$, the series and integral converge or diverge together.

content.caption: The Integral Test: comparing series sums to integrals using rectangles.
content.alt: Two side-by-side graphs illustrating the Integral Test. Both show a decreasing curve y equals f of x with rectangles of width 1 at integer x-values. Left graph shows left-endpoint rectangles (heights at x equals 1, 2, 3, etc.) which overestimate the area under the curve. Right graph shows right-endpoint rectangles (heights at x equals 2, 3, 4, etc.) which underestimate the area. This demonstrates that the sum of the series and the improper integral converge or diverge together.

content.title: The Integral Test: convergence
content.solution:
Let $f(x) = \frac{1}{x^{2}}$. This is continuous, positive, and decreasing on
$[1,\infty)$. We compute:
\[
 \defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{2}}\dx
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\left[-\frac{1}{x}\right]_{1}^{b}
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\left(-\frac{1}{b} + 1\right) = 1.
\]
The integral converges, so by the Integral Test,
$\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{2}}$ **converges**.

content.title: The integral value $\neq$ the series sum
content.content:
In Example [ex:integral-test-conv], the integral equals $1$, but the series
sum is *not* $1$. The Integral Test tells us about convergence, not the
actual value. (Euler proved $\sum 1/n^{2} = \pi^{2}/6 \approx 1.645$.)

content.title: The Integral Test: divergence
content.solution:
Let $f(x) = x^{-1/2}$. Then:
\[
 \defint{1}{\infty}x^{-1/2}\dx
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\left[2\sqrt{x}\right]_{1}^{b}
 = \limto{b\to\infty}(2\sqrt{b} - 2) = \infty.
\]
The integral diverges, so by the Integral Test,
$\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$ **diverges**.

content.title: Verifying Integral Test hypotheses
content.problem:
Determine whether $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{3}}$ converges 
using the Integral Test.
content.solution:
Let $f(x) = \frac{1}{x^{3}}$. Check the hypotheses:

- **Continuous:** $f(x) = x^{-3}$ is continuous on $[1, \infty)$. 

- **Positive:** $f(x) > 0$ for $x \geq 1$. 

- **Decreasing:** $f'(x) = -3x^{-4} < 0$ for $x \geq 1$. 

Now evaluate the integral:
\[
\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{3}}\dx = \limto{b\to\infty}
\left[-\frac{1}{2x^{2}}\right]_{1}^{b} = \limto{b\to\infty}
\left(-\frac{1}{2b^{2}} + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}.
\]

The integral converges, so the series $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}
\frac{1}{n^{3}}$ **converges**.

content.text: The Integral Test immediately gives us a powerful family of examples.

content.content:
Apply the Integral Test with $f(x) = x^{-p}$. From Chapter 8, we know
$\defint{1}{\infty}x^{-p}\dx$ converges if and only if $p > 1$.

Specifically, for $p \neq 1$:
\[
\defint{1}{\infty}x^{-p}\dx = \limto{b\to\infty}\left[\frac{x^{1-p}}{1-p}
\right]_{1}^{b} = \frac{1}{1-p}\limto{b\to\infty}(b^{1-p} - 1).
\]

- If $p > 1$: $1 - p < 0$, so $b^{1-p} \to 0$. The integral equals 
$\frac{1}{p-1}$. **Converges**.

- If $p < 1$: $1 - p > 0$, so $b^{1-p} \to \infty$. **Diverges**.

For $p = 1$: $\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x}\dx = \limto{b\to\infty}\ln b 
= \infty$. **Diverges**.

content.title: $p$-series examples
content.solution:
**(a)** $p = 3 > 1$, so the series **converges**.

**(b)** $p = 0.99 < 1$, so the series **diverges**.

**(c)** $p = 1$, so the series **diverges**. (This is the harmonic
series.)

content.title: More $p$-series applications
content.solution:
**(a)** $\frac{1}{\sqrt[3]{n^{2}}} = \frac{1}{n^{2/3}}$. Since 
$p = \frac{2}{3} < 1$, the series **diverges**.

**(b)** Since $\pi \approx 3.14 > 1$, the series **converges**.

**(c)** This is $5\sum\frac{1}{n^{1.01}}$. Since $p = 1.01 > 1$, 
$\sum\frac{1}{n^{1.01}}$ converges, so $5\sum\frac{1}{n^{1.01}}$ 
**converges**.

content.text:
The borderline case $p = 1$ (harmonic series) diverges. Any series with
terms decaying faster than $1/n$ *might* converge; any with terms
decaying more slowly *definitely* diverges.

content.title: Integral Test with logarithms
content.solution:
Let $f(x) = \frac{1}{x\ln x}$ for $x \geq 2$. This is positive, continuous,
and decreasing. Compute:
\[
 \defint{2}{\infty}\frac{1}{x\ln x}\dx
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\bigl[\ln(\ln x)\bigr]_{2}^{b}
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\bigl(\ln(\ln b) - \ln(\ln 2)\bigr) = \infty.
\]
The integral diverges, so the series **diverges**.

content.title: A convergent logarithmic series
content.solution:
Let $f(x) = \frac{1}{x(\ln x)^{2}}$. Substitute $u = \ln x$:
\[
 \defint{2}{\infty}\frac{1}{x(\ln x)^{2}}\dx
 = \defint{\ln 2}{\infty}\frac{1}{u^{2}}\du
 = \left[-\frac{1}{u}\right]_{\ln 2}^{\infty}
 = \frac{1}{\ln 2}.
\]
The integral converges, so the series **converges**.

content.title: Generalized logarithmic series
content.problem:
For what values of $p$ does $\displaystyle\sum_{n=2}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n(\ln n)^{p}}$ 
converge?
content.solution:
Let $f(x) = \frac{1}{x(\ln x)^{p}}$. Substitute $u = \ln x$:
\[
\defint{2}{\infty}\frac{1}{x(\ln x)^{p}}\dx = \defint{\ln 2}^{\infty}
\frac{1}{u^{p}}\du.
\]

This is a $p$-integral! It converges if and only if $p > 1$.

Therefore, $\displaystyle\sum_{n=2}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n(\ln n)^{p}}$ 
**converges if $p > 1$** and **diverges if $p \leq 1$**.

content.title: Integral Test with exponentials
content.solution:
Let $f(x) = xe^{-x^{2}}$. Check:

- Continuous and positive on $[1, \infty)$. 

- Decreasing: $f'(x) = e^{-x^{2}} - 2x^{2}e^{-x^{2}} = (1-2x^{2})e^{-x^{2}} < 0$
for $x > 1/\sqrt{2}$. 

Evaluate using substitution $u = x^{2}$:
\[
\defint{1}{\infty}xe^{-x^{2}}\dx = \frac{1}{2}\defint{1}{\infty}e^{-u}\du
= \frac{1}{2}\left[-e^{-u}\right]_{1}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{2}e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e}.
\]

The integral converges, so the series **converges**.

content.title: Integral Test with arctangent
content.solution:
Let $f(x) = \frac{1}{x^{2}+1}$. This is positive, continuous, and 
decreasing on $[1, \infty)$.

\[
\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{2}+1}\dx = \left[\arctan x\right]_{1}^{\infty}
= \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4}.
\]

The integral converges, so the series **converges**.

content.text: Estimating Sums with the Integral Test

The Integral Test not only determines convergence but also provides
*bounds* for the sum.

content.title: Remainder Estimate for the Integral Test
content.content:
Let $\sum a_{n}$ be a convergent series to which the Integral Test applies.
If $R_{N} = \sum_{n=N+1}^{\infty}a_{n}$ is the remainder after $N$ terms, then
\[
 \defint{N+1}{\infty}f(x)\dx \leq R_{N} \leq \defint{N}{\infty}f(x)\dx.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text: This tells us how many terms to add for a desired accuracy.

content.title: Estimating the sum of $1/n^{2}$
content.solution:
We need $R_{N} < 0.001$. Using the upper bound:
\[
 R_{N} \leq \defint{N}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{2}}\dx
 = \left[-\frac{1}{x}\right]_{N}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{N}.
\]
So $R_{N} < 0.001$ when $N > 1000$. Computing:
\[
 s_{1000} = \sum_{n=1}^{1000}\frac{1}{n^{2}} \approx 1.6439.
\]
The error is at most $1/1000 = 0.001$, so the sum is between $1.6429$ and
$1.6449$. (The exact value is $\pi^{2}/6 \approx 1.6449$.)

content.title: Better estimate using bounds
content.problem: Use $s_{10}$ and remainder bounds to estimate $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{2}}$.
content.solution:
Compute $s_{10} = 1 + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{9} + \cdots + \frac{1}{100}
\approx 1.5498$. The remainder satisfies:
\[
 \defint{11}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{2}}\dx \leq R_{10}
 \leq \defint{10}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{2}}\dx,
\]
i.e., $\frac{1}{11} \leq R_{10} \leq \frac{1}{10}$, or
$0.0909 \leq R_{10} \leq 0.1$. Thus:
\[
 s_{10} + \frac{1}{11} \leq S \leq s_{10} + \frac{1}{10},
\]
giving $1.6407 \leq S \leq 1.6498$. Averaging: $S \approx 1.645$.

content.title: Estimating $\sum 1/n^{3}$
content.solution:
We need $R_{N} < 0.01$. Using the upper bound:
\[
R_{N} \leq \defint{N}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{3}}\dx = 
\left[-\frac{1}{2x^{2}}\right]_{N}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{2N^{2}}.
\]

We need $\frac{1}{2N^{2}} < 0.01$, so $N^{2} > 50$, giving $N > 7.07$. 
Take $N = 8$.

Computing:
\[
s_{8} = 1 + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{27} + \frac{1}{64} + \frac{1}{125} + 
\frac{1}{216} + \frac{1}{343} + \frac{1}{512} \approx 1.1952.
\]

The error is at most $\frac{1}{2(64)} = \frac{1}{128} \approx 0.0078 < 0.01$.

So $\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{3}} \approx 1.195$ with error 
less than $0.01$.

(The exact value is $\zeta(3) \approx 1.2021$, called Apéry's constant.)

content.title: Improving estimates with midpoint
content.problem:
Use the remainder bounds to get a better estimate of 
$\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{2}}$ using only $s_{5}$.
content.solution:
Compute $s_{5} = 1 + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{16} + \frac{1}{25} 
= \frac{5269}{3600} \approx 1.4636$.

The remainder bounds give:
\[
\frac{1}{6} \leq R_{5} \leq \frac{1}{5}.
\]

So $1.4636 + 0.1667 \leq S \leq 1.4636 + 0.2$, giving 
$1.6303 \leq S \leq 1.6636$.

Taking the midpoint of the bounds: $R_{5} \approx \frac{1}{2}(\frac{1}{5} + 
\frac{1}{6}) = \frac{11}{60} \approx 0.1833$.

Better estimate: $S \approx 1.4636 + 0.1833 = 1.6469$.

(The exact value is $\pi^{2}/6 \approx 1.6449$. Our estimate has error 
about $0.002$!)

content.title: Euler and the Basel Problem
content.content:
In 1735, Leonhard Euler solved the "Basel problem" that had frustrated
mathematicians for nearly a century: finding the exact sum
$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}1/n^{2}$. His answer, $\pi^{2}/6$, stunned the mathematical
world. Euler went on to find the sums $\sum 1/n^{4} = \pi^{4}/90$,
$\sum 1/n^{6} = \pi^{6}/945$, and so on for all even powers. The odd powers
remain mysterious: no one has found a closed form for $\sum 1/n^{3}$ (called
Apéry's constant, $\zeta(3) \approx 1.2021$), though Roger Apéry proved in 
1978 that it is irrational.

Euler saw mathematics as revealing the structure of God's creation. He once
wrote, "For since the fabric of the universe is most perfect, and is the work
of a most wise Creator, nothing whatsoever takes place in the universe in
which some relation of maximum and minimum does not appear." His religious
faith and mathematical genius were intertwined.

content.title: Combining tests
content.solution:
First, apply the Divergence Test:
$\limto{n\to\infty}\frac{n}{n^{2}+1} = \limto{n\to\infty}\frac{1}{n + 1/n} = 0$.
Inconclusive.

Now try the Integral Test. Let $f(x) = \frac{x}{x^{2}+1}$. This is positive 
and continuous. To check if it's decreasing, compute:
\[
f'(x) = \frac{(x^{2}+1) - x(2x)}{(x^{2}+1)^{2}} = \frac{1 - x^{2}}{(x^{2}+1)^{2}}.
\]
$f'(x) < 0$ when $x > 1$, so $f$ is decreasing on $[1, \infty)$.

Evaluate the integral:
\[
\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{x}{x^{2}+1}\dx = \frac{1}{2}\left[\ln(x^{2}+1)
\right]_{1}^{\infty} = \infty.
\]

The integral diverges, so the series **diverges**.

content.title: A tricky integral
content.solution:
Let $f(x) = \frac{1}{x\ln x \cdot \ln(\ln x)}$ for $x \geq 3$. (We start 
at $n = 3$ to ensure $\ln(\ln x) > 0$.)

Substitute $u = \ln x$, then $v = \ln u$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\defint{3}{\infty}\frac{1}{x\ln x \cdot \ln(\ln x)}\dx 
&= \defint{\ln 3}{\infty}\frac{1}{u \cdot \ln u}\du \\
&= \defint{\ln(\ln 3)}^{\infty}\frac{1}{v}\dv = \infty.
\end{aligned}$$

The integral diverges, so the series **diverges**.

content.title: Double logarithm series
content.solution:
Let $f(x) = \frac{1}{x\ln x \cdot (\ln(\ln x))^{2}}$.

Using the same substitutions as above:
$$\begin{aligned}
\defint{3}{\infty}\frac{1}{x\ln x \cdot (\ln(\ln x))^{2}}\dx 
&= \defint{\ln(\ln 3)}^{\infty}\frac{1}{v^{2}}\dv \\
&= \left[-\frac{1}{v}\right]_{\ln(\ln 3)}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\ln(\ln 3)}.
\end{aligned}$$

The integral converges, so the series **converges**.

marginNotes.text:
There is a way that seems right to a man,
but its end is the way to death.

Beloved, do not believe every spirit, but test 
the spirits to see whether they are from God.

For his invisible attributes, namely, his
eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the
creation of the world, in the things that have been made.

How precious to me are your thoughts, O
God! How vast is the sum of them! If I would count them, they are more
than the sand.

It is the glory of God to conceal things, 
but the glory of kings is to search things out.

For which of you, desiring to build a tower,
does not first sit down and count the cost?

For my thoughts are not your thoughts, 
neither are your ways my ways, declares the Lord. For as the heavens are 
higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways and my thoughts 
than your thoughts.

marginNotes.author: Paul Erdős
marginNotes.text:
A mathematician is a device for turning coffee into
theorems.

marginNotes.author: Carl Friedrich Gauss
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is the queen of the sciences, 
and number theory is the queen of mathematics.

marginNotes.author: Pierre-Simon Laplace
marginNotes.text:
What we know is not much. What we do not
know is immense.
